text: The robotic low anterior resection procedure begins with the surgeon using six ports, including one positioned for splenic flexure mobilization. The approach is from medial to lateral.
The surgeon clears the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) of fibrolymphatic structures. The surgical field shows the ureter visible behind the mesocolon as the procedure continues.

The surgeon secures and transects the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The pelvic nerves are visible, and the Gerota's fascia is pushed down. The pancreas is visualized as a bulge, with the transverse mesocolon running over it, which is then transected. The inferior mesenteric vein is secured.
The surgeon completes the splenic flexure mobilization by dissecting the gastrocolic omentum and freeing the splenic flexure from its attachments.
The robotic arms two and three are repositioned, and the pelvic dissection continues. The ureter, pelvic nerve, and gonadal vessels are visible, along with the ongoing mesorectal dissection.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection of the rectum, encountering severe adhesions in the anterior aspect due to the patient's previous abdominal hysterectomy.
The surgeon focuses on the posterior aspect of the dissection, with the pelvic floor clearly visualized in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection, clearing fibro fatty tissue from the posterior wall of the vagina. The glistening white layer of the vaginal wall is visible as the dissection progresses towards the pelvic floor.
The surgeon demonstrates the completely mobilized rectum, which is transected using a green stapler. An anastomosis is then performed between the rectal stump and the colon.
